column2: The function of UX Design involves making websites, apps, or products easy and enjoyable to use by planning how they look and work, understanding what users need through research, creating visual designs, and coming up with strategies to improve the overall experience for people who use them.
column3: The UX Design function is responsible for activities like creating clickable prototypes, wireframes, storyboards, and customer experience maps, as well as conducting research through interviews, surveys, usability testing, and analyzing research findings. This function is accountable for making sure these activities are done well and on time, and is often consulted for input on research methods, ideation, and competitor analysis. The function is informed by analytics, stakeholder feedback, and project requirements. To do this, the function uses skills like creativity, critical thinking, problem-solving, and collaboration, applying practices such as agility, decision-making, and continuous growth. For example, when making a prototype, the function uses design thinking and attention to detail, and when running a usability test, it uses empathy, communication, and analytical skills to understand user needs and improve the design.
column4: The daily deliverables for the UX Design function include low- and high-fidelity prototypes, wireframes, UI development specifications, design annotations, research reports, user journey maps, and competitive analysis documents. The function is responsible and accountable for producing these deliverables, ensuring they meet quality standards and support the overall user experience strategy. The function is consulted on deliverables like research analysis, usability test reports, and affinity maps, and is informed by documents such as stakeholder feedback, project plans, and analytics audits. To create these deliverables, the function applies skills like information visualization, design systems management, and written communication, while practicing ownership, empowerment, and service leadership. For instance, when preparing a research report, the function organizes findings clearly and presents them to others, using storytelling and presentation skills to make the information easy to understand and actionable.
column5: The UX Design function is different from other functions because it focuses on understanding users’ needs and creating solutions that make digital products easy and enjoyable to use, using skills like creativity, critical thinking, and empathy. This function’s daily activities include making wireframes, clickable prototypes, and storyboards, mapping customer experiences, and conducting research through interviews, surveys, and usability tests, while analyzing findings to improve designs. The function is responsible and accountable for producing deliverables such as prototypes, wireframes, user journey maps, research reports, and design annotations, ensuring these meet quality standards and support the user experience strategy. It is consulted for input on research methods, ideation, and competitor analysis, and is informed by analytics, stakeholder feedback, and project requirements. Unlike other functions that may focus on coding, marketing, or business strategy, the UX Design function’s practices center on agility, decision-making, and continuous growth, and its daily deliverables and activities are all aimed at making products more user-friendly and effective.

column2: The function of UX Research involves finding out how people use websites or apps by observing their actions, asking questions, and collecting feedback, so that designers can make digital products easier and more enjoyable to use. UX Strategy means planning how to improve the overall experience for users by setting goals, understanding what users need, and making decisions that help create better products.
column3: The UX Research function spends each day planning and running studies like interviews, surveys, usability tests, and card sorts to understand what users need and how they interact with products. This function chooses the right research methods, creates research plans, observes users, collects and analyzes data, and presents findings. It is responsible for moderating research sessions, identifying patterns, and testing prototypes. The function consults with others to gather requirements, define research operations, and refine strategies, and is informed by analytics, competitor investigations, and feedback from other duties. Skills like critical thinking, data analysis, empathy, communication, and decision-making are used to design effective studies, interpret results, and share insights. Practices such as agility, ownership, and continuous growth help the function adapt to new challenges and improve research quality.
column4: The daily deliverables for the UX Research function include research reports, analysis documents, usability test reports, affinity maps, empathy maps, user journey maps, wireframes, prototypes, and strategy definitions. The function is responsible and accountable for creating these deliverables, ensuring they are clear, actionable, and based on solid evidence. It consults on deliverables like personas, competitive analyses, and customer experience diagrams, and is informed by project plans, KPIs, and stakeholder feedback. Skills such as storytelling, presentation, organization, and written communication are applied to make research findings understandable and useful for others. Practices like service leadership and psychological safety ensure that deliverables support better decision-making and foster collaboration across different duties.
column5: The UX Research function is different from other duties because it focuses on understanding users’ needs and behaviors through activities like interviews, surveys, usability tests, and data analysis, using skills such as empathy, critical thinking, and communication to design and run studies, interpret results, and share insights. Each day, this function is responsible and accountable for planning research, moderating sessions, analyzing findings, and creating deliverables like research reports, usability test reports, affinity maps, empathy maps, user journey maps, wireframes, prototypes, and strategy definitions, ensuring these are clear, actionable, and evidence-based. The function consults with other duties to gather requirements, refine strategies, and define research operations, and is informed by analytics, competitor investigations, and feedback. Unlike other duties that may focus on building, coding, or marketing, the UX Research function’s daily practices—such as agility, ownership, and continuous growth—center on discovering what users need and ensuring that products are designed to meet those needs, making it responsible and accountable for research activities and deliverables, consulted on related strategy and design work, and informed by project and stakeholder updates.

column2: The UX Writing function involves creating clear and helpful words that appear in apps, websites, or digital products to guide users and make their experience easy and enjoyable. This duty includes choosing the right words for buttons, instructions, and messages so people know what to do next. It also involves understanding what users need, researching how they interact with the product, and planning ways to make the experience smoother and more understandable for everyone.
column3: The UX Writing function spends each day inventorying content, shaping brand identity, writing clear and helpful copy, and storyboarding user flows. This function rapidly generates ideas, optimizes content for search engines, and maps out the customer experience. It creates and tests prototypes, analyzes research findings, and chooses the best research methods, such as interviews, surveys, and usability tests. The function consults with others to gather feedback, refine requirements, and ensure the content fits the overall strategy. Skills like attention to detail, creativity, critical thinking, and collaboration are used to make sure every word and message supports the user’s needs and the brand’s goals. Practices like agility, ownership, and continuous growth help the function adapt quickly, make informed decisions, and empower others to contribute ideas.
column4: The daily deliverables for the UX Writing function include content inventories, outlines, brand identity documents, editorial style guides, and polished copywriting documents. It produces microcopy, storyboards, strategy definitions, SEO strategies, customer journey maps, prototypes, research reports, and usability test reports. The function is responsible for ensuring these deliverables are clear, consistent, and user-focused, often consulting with others to gather insights and feedback. Skills such as written communication, organization, problem-solving, and stakeholder management are applied to create deliverables that guide design and development, support decision-making, and improve the overall user experience. Practices like service leadership and psychological safety ensure that everyone’s input is valued and that the deliverables meet both user and business needs.
column5: The UX Writing function is different from other duties because it focuses on shaping the words and messages users see and interact with, making sure every piece of content is clear, helpful, and matches the brand’s identity. This function’s daily activities include inventorying content, forming brand identity, writing and editing copy, storyboarding user flows, and mapping out the customer experience, while also testing prototypes and analyzing research findings to improve user understanding. The function is responsible and accountable for creating deliverables like content inventories, style guides, microcopy, storyboards, and research reports, ensuring they are user-focused and consistent. It consults with others to gather feedback and refine requirements, and keeps everyone informed about research findings and content decisions. Key skills include attention to detail, creativity, critical thinking, and collaboration, while practices like agility, ownership, and continuous growth help the function adapt quickly and make informed decisions, setting it apart from duties that focus more on design, coding, or business strategy.

column2: The Project Operations function involves making sure that projects run smoothly from start to finish by organizing tasks, keeping track of progress, helping everyone work together, and solving problems as they come up. Duties include guiding groups through their work process (Scrum Master), planning and tracking project goals (Project Management), keeping in touch with clients to make sure their needs are met (Account Management), and making sure products are developed and delivered the right way (Product Operations).
column3: The Project Operations function is responsible for organizing and running daily activities like planning sprints, leading retrospectives, facilitating demos, tracking progress, estimating effort, calculating velocity, and running agile workshops. This function also analyzes project risks, schedules meetings, tracks milestones, maintains client relationships, defines working agreements, sets up processes, clarifies duties, chooses agile methods, and leads agile transformation. To do this well, the function uses skills like organization, communication, problem-solving, and decision-making. It practices agility by adapting to changes, empowers others by encouraging input, and ensures psychological safety by making it safe to share ideas. The function collaborates with others by consulting on requirements, user stories, and risk analysis, and keeps everyone informed about progress and changes.
column4: The Project Operations function is responsible for deliverables such as sprint goals, sprint plans, retro action items, agile maturity measurements, demo presentations, refined backlogs, burn down charts, workshop results, project plans, risk analyses, meeting agendas, roadmaps, Gantt charts, working agreements, psychological safety plans, process maps, Kanban board statuses, RACI charts, and team charters. The function is accountable for making sure these deliverables are accurate, up-to-date, and useful for everyone involved. It applies skills like attention to detail, prioritization, and project management to create and maintain these deliverables. The function consults with others to gather input and feedback, and informs others by sharing updates and results, using clear communication and collaboration to keep everyone aligned and moving forward.
column5: The Project Operations function differs from other functions because it focuses on organizing and running the daily flow of work, like planning sprints, leading retrospectives, and tracking progress, while also making sure everyone is clear on their duties and processes. This function uses skills like organization, communication, and problem-solving to create deliverables such as sprint plans, burn down charts, and project roadmaps, and is responsible and accountable for keeping these accurate and useful. The function consults with others to gather input on requirements, risks, and user stories, and keeps everyone informed about progress and changes, practicing agility, empowerment, and psychological safety to help everyone work better together. Unlike other functions that may focus on building products or coding, Project Operations centers on planning, coordination, and making sure work runs smoothly and transparently for everyone involved.

column2: The function of Product Ownership involves making important decisions about what a product should do and how it should work, making sure it solves real problems for people who use it. This duty includes understanding what customers need, deciding which features are most important, and working closely with others to make sure the product is built the right way. Product Management is the duty of planning how a product will grow and improve over time, setting goals, and making sure everyone is working toward the same vision. Business Analysis is the duty of studying information and feedback to figure out what changes or improvements will make the product more useful and successful.
column3: The Product Ownership function spends each day maintaining and refining the product backlog, identifying priorities, planning sprints, researching competitors, defining feature and release scope, and tracking progress on large initiatives. This function is responsible for gathering and refining requirements, writing user stories, creating acceptance criteria, and generating problem statements. It also defines the product vision and strategy, develops go-to-market plans, segments customers, and positions the product in the market. The function is accountable for conducting contextual inquiries, creating research plans, collecting analytics, facilitating sprint demos, and ensuring quality through user acceptance testing and QA. It is consulted in research, risk analysis, UX design, and technical architecture, and is informed by analytics, research findings, and feedback. To do this well, the function uses skills like backlog management, prioritization, critical thinking, collaboration, and decision-making, while practicing agility, ownership, and continuous growth. Communication, negotiation, and facilitation are key to working with others, ensuring everyone is aligned and empowered.
column4: The daily deliverables for the Product Ownership function include a refined and prioritized backlog, sprint goals and plans, competitive analysis, acceptance criteria, product roadmaps, release plans, vision boards, and go-to-market strategies. The function is responsible for creating user personas, empathy maps, user journey maps, market research analysis, user stories, problem statements, use case scenarios, and project plans. It is accountable for research reports, QA test cases, code documentation, architecture diagrams, and brand assets. The function is consulted on prototypes, risk analysis, research reports, and marketing plans, and is informed by analytics audits, usability test reports, and stakeholder feedback. To deliver these, the function applies skills in requirements management, data analysis, storytelling, and strategic thinking, while practicing empowerment, psychological safety, and service leadership. This ensures that deliverables are clear, actionable, and support the overall product vision, while keeping everyone informed and engaged in the process.
column5: The Product Ownership function is different from other duties because it focuses on defining what should be built, why it matters, and how it fits into the bigger picture, using skills like backlog management, prioritization, and strategic thinking. Each day, this function maintains and refines the product backlog, sets priorities, plans sprints, researches competitors, and defines the scope and vision for new features and releases. It is responsible for gathering and refining requirements, writing user stories, and creating acceptance criteria, while also developing go-to-market strategies and positioning the product in the market. The function is accountable for research, analytics, sprint demos, and ensuring quality through user acceptance testing and QA. It is consulted on research, risk analysis, UX design, and technical architecture, and is informed by analytics, research findings, and feedback. Daily deliverables include a refined backlog, sprint plans, competitive analysis, product roadmaps, release plans, user personas, and market research analysis. This function practices agility, ownership, and continuous growth, and uses communication, negotiation, and facilitation to keep everyone aligned and empowered, making it unique from other duties that may focus more on execution, technical delivery, or support.

column2: The engineering function involves creating and improving technology by building software or hardware (development), checking that everything works correctly and safely (QA analysis), protecting information from hackers and other threats (information security analysis), and designing the best ways for different systems to work together smoothly (solutions architecture).
column3: The engineering function in this context is responsible and accountable for activities like blockchain coding, creating REST APIs, building and documenting databases, deploying and documenting code, front-end and server-side coding, creating autotests, QA testing, firewall configuration, information security audits, penetration testing, defining security protocols, threat modeling, entity relationship creation, and technical architecture creation. This function is also consulted in activities such as generating problem statements, user story generation, requirements gathering and refinement, coaching others, estimating effort, facilitating agile workshops, and defining team processes. The function is informed by activities like analyzing project risk, collecting analytics, presenting research results, and tracking progress. To perform these activities, the function uses skills like problem-solving, critical thinking, attention to detail, collaboration, communication, and technical expertise in programming, database management, and information security. Practices such as continuous growth, decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety are applied by sharing knowledge, seeking feedback, and working closely with others to ensure high-quality outcomes.
column4: The daily deliverables for this function include code, smart contracts, database architecture, entity relationship diagrams, coding documentation, API libraries, QA test cases and plans, QA analysis, network and system architecture diagrams, hosting and microservices infrastructure, and technical architecture specifications. The function is also consulted on deliverables like problem statements, user stories, acceptance criteria, project plans, KPIs, backlog refinement, and prototypes, and is informed by deliverables such as project risk analysis, customer experience diagrams, and market research analysis. To create and maintain these deliverables, the function applies skills in technical writing, database design, API development, QA planning, and architectural thinking, while practicing ownership, empowerment, and service leadership. Collaboration and communication are key, as the function works with others to gather requirements, refine solutions, and ensure that deliverables meet both technical and business needs.
column5: The engineering function differs from other job functions because its duties focus on building, testing, and securing technology systems through activities like coding, database design, API development, QA testing, and security analysis, requiring strong technical skills, problem-solving, and attention to detail. This function is responsible and accountable for creating and maintaining technical deliverables such as code, smart contracts, architecture diagrams, and QA plans, while being consulted on requirements, user stories, and project planning, and informed about project risks, analytics, and research findings. Daily practices include continuous learning, ownership, and collaboration, with the function applying technical expertise and communication skills to ensure high-quality, secure, and reliable technology solutions, while working closely with others to align technical work with business needs.

column2: The function of Agile Coaching involves helping people learn better ways to work together, solve problems, and adapt to changes quickly. This duty means guiding others to use simple steps to plan, check progress, and improve how they get things done, so everyone can deliver results faster and with less stress.
column3: The Agile Coach function guides others in adopting agile ways of working by coaching on effective collaboration, estimating effort, setting working agreements, facilitating workshops, planning sprints, running retrospectives, defining duties and responsibilities, tracking progress, and choosing the best agile approach. This function uses skills like coaching, facilitation, communication, and conflict resolution to help others grow, make decisions, and feel safe to share ideas. By practicing agility, continuous growth, and empowerment, the function encourages open feedback, creative problem-solving, and shared ownership of outcomes.
column4: The Agile Coach function is responsible for creating and maintaining a personal growth roadmap and is accountable for deliverables such as sprint goals, refined backlogs, working agreements, psychological safety plans, workshop results, sprint plans, retro action items, agile maturity measurements, RACI charts, burn down charts, agile methods, process maps, and charters. This function consults on process maps, board statuses, growth roadmaps, and more, and is informed by a wide range of project and research activities. Applying attention to detail, organization, and strategic thinking, the function ensures that deliverables are clear, actionable, and support continuous improvement, while fostering a culture of feedback, empowerment, and shared responsibility.
column5: The Agile Coach function differs from other duties because it focuses on guiding others to adopt agile ways of working by coaching on collaboration, estimating effort, setting working agreements, facilitating workshops, planning sprints, running retrospectives, defining duties and responsibilities, tracking progress, and choosing the best agile approach. This function uses skills like coaching, facilitation, communication, and conflict resolution to help others grow, make decisions, and feel safe to share ideas. Daily activities include creating and maintaining a personal growth roadmap and being accountable for deliverables such as sprint goals, refined backlogs, working agreements, psychological safety plans, workshop results, sprint plans, retro action items, agile maturity measurements, RACI charts, burn down charts, agile methods, process maps, and charters. The function is responsible for coaching others, accountable for estimating effort, defining working agreements, facilitating workshops, planning sprints, running retrospectives, defining duties, tracking progress, calculating velocity, choosing agile methods, and leading agile transformation, while consulting on process maps, board statuses, growth roadmaps, and more, and being informed by a wide range of project and research activities. This function stands out by practicing agility, continuous growth, empowerment, and psychological safety, ensuring that deliverables are clear, actionable, and support continuous improvement, while fostering a culture of feedback, empowerment, and shared responsibility.
